input.content:
# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion

Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).

`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
on. Two goals:

## 1. Parity

por and dual should be first-class equals across every
subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.

## 2. Bidirectional conversion

A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
shapes in place.

- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
  cross-links going forward.
- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.

## Open questions (resolve at design time)

- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
  `dual → por`.
- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
  interrupted mid-way.

## See also

- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions
still open.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

Merge direction and chores-NN ordering moved to
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (top entries) for
discoverability. Remaining technical question:

- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions taken
(see Resolution below).

## Resolution (2026-05-11)

`init-clone-refactor` landed into `main` at `0.42.0-4.7`
(see `notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`): `main`'s `0.42.0-0..-4.6` work was
rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1` close-out tip,
so the branch's commits are now ancestors of `main`. The
branch has since been deleted locally and remotely.

The "still deferred" / "still open" wording in the dated
sections below is superseded — the merge-direction question
is resolved (rebase-main-onto-branch), and the chores-NN
ordering follow-up survives only as a low-priority entry in
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo`. This file is retained as a
historical post-mortem; the recovery playbook and
diagnostic techniques remain reusable.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

Merge direction and chores-NN ordering moved to
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (top entries) for
discoverability. Remaining technical question:

- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

-  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
-  surface only at the rebase step.

+# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion
+
+Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
+lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
+`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).
+
+`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
+plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
+trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
+shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
+`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
+on. Two goals:
+
+## 1. Parity
+
+por and dual should be first-class equals across every
+subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
+"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
+rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
+`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
+substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.
+
+## 2. Bidirectional conversion
+
+A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
+shapes in place.
+
+- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
+  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
+  cross-links going forward.
+- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
+  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.
+
+## Open questions (resolve at design time)
+
+- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
+  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
+- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
+  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
+  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
+- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
+  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
+  `dual → por`.
+- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
+  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
+- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
+  interrupted mid-way.
+
+## See also
+
+- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
+  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
+- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
+  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
+- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
+  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
+  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

+# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion
+
+Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
+lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
+`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).
+
+`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
+plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
+trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
+shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
+`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
+on. Two goals:
+
+## 1. Parity
+
+por and dual should be first-class equals across every
+subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
+"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
+rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
+`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
+substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.
+
+## 2. Bidirectional conversion
+
+A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
+shapes in place.
+
+- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
+  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
+  cross-links going forward.
+- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
+  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.
+
+## Open questions (resolve at design time)
+
+- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
+  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
+- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
+  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
+  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
+- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
+  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
+  `dual → por`.
+- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
+  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
+- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
+  interrupted mid-way.
+
+## See also
+
+- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
+  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
+- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
+  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
+- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
+  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
+  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

content.text:
You're right, and the principle's correct — `por-dual-parity.md` is the source of truth; everything else should *point*, not *copy*. Let me sharpen which of those `rg` hits are actually a problem, though, because they're not all the same:

- **`todo.md` line 45 — real duplication.** The `## Todo` item is a 10-line paragraph that re-states the stub's content. The project's own todo-format rule (`notes/README.md` / CLAUDE.md: "Detailed motivation … belong in `notes/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.") says this should be a terse pointer. **Fix: trim it.**
- **`init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` line 239 — soft dup.** It quotes the todo item's *title* verbatim. Not content duplication, but unnecessary — it can just say "a low-priority entry in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`". **Minor tweak.**
- **`chores-09.md` lines 979 / 991 / 995 — not duplication.** That subsection is the *record of what the 0.48.1 commit did*; naming the artifacts it touched ("new `## Todo` item 'por/dual parity…'", "reworded the 'Cross-file chores-NN…' item") is exactly an Edits list's job. The *phrase* recurring there is unavoidable and correct — it's describing, not defining. **Leave it.**
- **`todo.md` line 247 (`## Done`) — just a title.** Fine.

So: one real fix (trim the todo item), one cosmetic (drop the quoted title in the icr doc), plus I'll lowercase the `por-dual-parity.md` H1 to match how it's written everywhere else (`# por/dual parity + bidirectional conversion`). Applying now:

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions taken
(see Resolution below).

## Resolution (2026-05-11)

`init-clone-refactor` landed into `main` at `0.42.0-4.7`
(see `notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`): `main`'s `0.42.0-0..-4.6` work was
rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1` close-out tip,
so the branch's commits are now ancestors of `main`. The
branch has since been deleted locally and remotely.

The "still deferred" / "still open" wording in the dated
sections below is superseded — the merge-direction question
is resolved (rebase-main-onto-branch), and the chores-NN
ordering follow-up survives only as a low-priority entry in
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo`. This file is retained as a
historical post-mortem; the recovery playbook and
diagnostic techniques remain reusable.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (resolved 2026-05-11)

The merge-direction call, once the recovered branch
gave a clean base to choose from:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Resolved: the "vice versa" topology — `main`'s
`0.42.0-0..-4.6` work rebased on top of the branch at
its `0.41.1` close-out tip, force-pushed at close-out
(`0.42.0-4.7`). See Resolution above and
`notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions (resolved — see Resolution above)

All resolved by the `0.42.0-4.7` rebase landing:

- **Merge direction** — settled: `main`'s `0.42.0-X`
  work rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1`
  close-out tip.
- **chores-NN ordering** — survives only as a
  low-priority "Cross-file `chores-NN.md` ordering
  sanity pass" entry in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`.
- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0** — the real
  conflicts (`Cargo.toml`, `notes/todo.md`,
  `notes/chores-08.md`, CLAUDE.md, `src/init.rs`,
  `src/scope.rs`, `src/common.rs`) surfaced and were
  resolved during the cascade; see
  `notes/chores-09.md > ### What needed real work`.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion

Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).

`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
on. Two goals:

## 1. Parity

por and dual should be first-class equals across every
subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.

## 2. Bidirectional conversion

A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
shapes in place.

- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
  cross-links going forward.
- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.

## Open questions (resolve at design time)

- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
  `dual → por`.
- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
  interrupted mid-way.

## See also

- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

DRY pass applied. Final state of the structure:

- **`notes/por-dual-parity.md`** — the single source of truth for the *goal* (parity, `por → dual` / `dual → por`, open questions). H1 lowercased to match the topic phrasing.
- **`notes/todo.md`** — the por/dual `## Todo` item is now a 5-line pointer (was 10): names the task, says it builds on the `--scope` rollout, defers the substance to `[91]`. (`## Done` entry and the chores `[92]` ref unchanged.)
- **`notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`** — the "Open questions (resolved)" bullet no longer quotes the todo item's title, just points at `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`.
- **`notes/chores-09.md`** — left as-is on purpose: that subsection is the *record of the 0.48.1 commit*, so naming the artifacts it touched ("new `## Todo` item …", "reworded the 'Cross-file chores-NN …' item") is the Edits-list's job, not content duplication.

The remaining "Cross-file `chores-NN.md` ordering" hits: the only one with *substance* is the `## Todo` item itself (its source of truth); the icr-doc and chores-09 references just point at / name it. The `forks-multi-user.md` and `chores-06.md` "cross-file" hits are unrelated (different context).

message.content:
No need for `# Edits` section in `chores-xx.md` that information will be in the
commit message and it should be the source of truth. Two suggestions:
1) The section title in a chores-xx.md file should exactly match the name of the commit.
   Of course the initial section title will probably be wrong, but at the time the
   commit is created one last edit is made and the section title is updated along
   with every reference to it!
2) When starting another change for the same repo grab the commits link, such as:
   https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/e284505c346ca051e03d36889389e49229252904
   and update the chores-xx.md section to have that link in a list of one or more references
   to the commits, something like ; `Commits: [23],[25]`

Thoughts

content.text:
Strong yes on the direction. The principle I'd state it as: **git is the source of truth for "what changed mechanically"; `chores-NN.md` is the source of truth for "why, and the design thinking"; the two are cross-linked, neither restates the other.** Today's setup has the *commit body* mirror the chores `### Edits` list — same content, two places, and the duplication CLAUDE.md acknowledges but resolves the wrong way (it names the markdown the truth; the immutable, tooled, naturally-commit-scoped record is the better home). Your three points fix exactly that. Per-point:

**1) Drop `### Edits` from `chores-NN.md` — yes.** What stays in a chores section: the intro paragraph (now pitched a notch higher — "this step ports X to Context, no behavior change" rather than a file list), the `###` design subsections (the durable rationale a commit message is the wrong place for), and the new `Commits:` pointer. The one cost: to see "which files," a markdown reader takes one hop (click the commit link) instead of reading inline — acceptable, since CLAUDE.md already requires the commit *body* to carry the curated "file + one-line gist" list, so nothing is lost, it just lives in the one place that describes that commit. (`.claude`-side commits are unaffected — they never had the dup.)

**2) Chores heading === commit title, synced at finalize — yes, with eyes open.** Real costs:
- The heading inherits the commit-title form: `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, ≤50 chars. So `## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)` replaces today's freer `## push → Context + PushParams (0.48.0-6)`. The descriptive richness (arrow notation, type names) moves into the intro paragraph; the heading's job becomes *identify*, not *describe*.
- "Update every reference to it" = every `[N]: /notes/chores-NN.md#anchor` back-ref (in `todo.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, sibling chores files, the file itself) gets re-pointed when the heading changes. That's the "one last edit." It needs to be a pre-commit-checklist item, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should check anchor integrity (dangling `#anchor` refs are the failure mode).
- Timing slots into the existing flow cleanly: do the work → draft the chores section with a provisional heading → at the message-approval gate, settle the final title → edit the chores heading + back-refs to match → `vc-x1 push <bm> --title "<that title>" --body "<the edit list>"`. The chores edit rides in the same commit it describes.

**3) `Commits: [N],[M]` ref list with the commit URL — yes.** Caveats:
- **One-commit lag.** The URL doesn't exist until `push-app` runs, so it gets backfilled in the *next* cycle's commit (your "when starting another change, grab the link" step). Accept it — the commit itself is the truth; the `Commits:` line is a browse-convenience, and a markdown reader who hits a not-yet-backfilled section just runs `git log --grep "(0.49.0-3)"`. Make "backfill the previous section's `Commits:` ref" a cycle-start checklist item. (Future: `vc-x1 push` could do the backfill itself right after `push-app`, since the SHA is known then — but that's a forward-looking enhancement, not a blocker.)
- **Host coupling.** `https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<sha>` rots if the repo moves. The project's already drifting toward GitHub URLs for refs (there's a todo for `blob/main/...` source refs), so it's consistent — but consider pairing it with the short SHA in the ref text so `git show <sha>` works host-independently: e.g. `[23]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/e284505c… (e284505c)`. Your call.
- Placement: a `Commits: [23]` line as the first line under the heading (metadata-style, immediately visible); the `[23]: <url>` target in the file's bottom `# References` section, using the existing machinery (`[23],[25]`, not `[23][25]`).

**Open sub-decisions before this lands:**
- **Retroactive scope.** Backfilling `Commits:` refs and renaming headings across `chores-01..08` is a lot of churn for little value — I'd say apply going forward only, maybe do `chores-09` as the worked example, leave 01–08 with their `### Edits` lists (harmless). Your call.
- **Squash interaction.** When a multi-step cycle lands *squashed to one commit*, do the per-substep subsections each point `Commits:` at that one squashed SHA (5 subsections → same SHA), or collapse the subsections too? Needs a rule.

**Scoping/sequencing.** This is a CLAUDE.md + `notes/README.md` convention change — it shouldn't ride the `0.49.0` code cycle. Options:
- **(a)** Amend it into the in-flight `0.48.1` (not pushed yet) — `0.48.1` becomes "icr cleanup + por/dual capture + chores convention," and itself drops its `### Edits` / renames its heading as the worked example. Fewest commits, but grows `0.48.1`'s scope.
- **(b)** Keep `0.48.1` as-is (reviewed, tight), do `0.48.2` next purely for the convention change — which forward-edits `0.48.1`'s chores subsection to the new shape as the demonstration. *My lean* — keeps each commit single-purpose.
- **(c)** Fold into `0.49.0-0` so the Migration B cycle's commits use the new convention from step one.

Which scoping ((a)/(b)/(c)), and your calls on retroactive scope + the squash rule? I'll write the CLAUDE.md/notes-README edits once you pick.

content.text:
Got it — locked in:
- **(b)** — `0.48.1` lands as-is (old shape, with `### Edits`); `0.48.2` is the convention cycle.
- **No retro** except `0.48.2` reshapes the `0.48.1` chores-09 section as the worked example — dropping its `### Edits`, renaming its heading to match the `0.48.1` commit title, adding the `Commits:` ref, and re-pointing any back-refs — so the cycle demonstrates the mechanics on a real section.
- **Squash** — ref/heading fixups are part of the squash work; that friction is a feature (discourages squashing; fine).

`0.48.2` shape (gets its own proper plan + approval once `0.48.1` is pushed): single-step — bump to `0.48.2`; CLAUDE.md edits (`### Chores section headers` / `### Chores: scannable bullets` → drop `### Edits`, heading === commit title, `Commits: [N]` refs; the squash note; pre-commit-checklist + cycle-start additions); `notes/README.md` to match; reshape the `0.48.1` chores-09 section retroactively; add the `0.48.2` chores-09 section born in the new shape (so the file ends with one converted section + one native-new section).

---

Per the workflow, `0.48.1` commits before we start `0.48.2`. Review's been done (the DRY pass settled it). Proposed commit message — same title/body for both repos, push handles the `ochid:` trailers:

**Title:**
```
docs: por-dual capture + icr cleanup (0.48.1)
```

**Body:**
```
Docs-only follow-on to the 0.48.0 close-out. Records the por/dual
parity + bidirectional-conversion goal as a forward-looking design
stub, and clears stale init-clone-refactor verbiage now that the
branch has landed in main (rebased at 0.42.0-4.7) and been deleted
local + remote. No code change.

- Cargo.toml: bump 0.48.0 → 0.48.1.

  — the parity goal, por→dual / dual→por conversion, open
  questions, see-also.
- notes/todo.md: add the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
  conversion" Todo item ([91]); drop the stale
  init-clone-refactor merge-direction + rebase-note items;
  reword the chores-NN ordering item to drop the icr framing;
  Done entry; [78] ref removed, [91]/[92] added.
- notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md: new "Resolution
  (2026-05-11)" section; intro / Strategic decision / Open
  questions past-tensed to match; dated post-mortem sections
  kept verbatim.
- notes/chores-09.md: new section recording this cycle.
```

`vc-x1 push main`'s preflight will run sync-check + fmt + clippy + test (`cargo build` is already green at 0.48.1). May I push?
